items.q: What is the Walker circulation and how does ENSO disrupt it?
items.correct: Walker circulation is the east-west tropical Pacific atmospheric loop: trade winds push warm water west, air rises in Indonesia, sinks over Peru. El Nino weakens it, La Nina strengthens it.
items.misconception: El Nino is a current that flows from Peru to Australia.
items.random_wrong: Walker circulation is the rotation of the Earth around the sun.
items.why: Students treat El Nino as a place or current; it's an oscillating state of a coupled ocean-atmosphere system. Walker circulation is the normal state — ENSO refers to its periodic flipping between phases.

items.q: Why is 'renewable' not the same as 'clean' energy?
items.correct: Renewable means the source replenishes naturally on human timescales; clean means low environmental/health impact. Hydropower is renewable but floods habitats; bioenergy is renewable but can drive deforestation.
items.misconception: All renewable energy sources are clean and have no environmental impact.
items.random_wrong: Clean energy means it doesn't produce any smoke when burned.
items.why: Large dams cause major ecological and social harm despite being renewable. Nuclear is low-carbon but not renewable. The two categories overlap but don't match — important for energy-policy evaluation.

items.q: What drives the movement of tectonic plates?
items.correct: Convection currents in the mantle combined with slab pull at subduction zones and ridge push at constructive boundaries.
items.misconception: The Earth's rotation makes the plates move.
items.random_wrong: Plates move because of ocean tides pulling on the crust.
items.why: Students conflate the Earth spinning with crustal motion. Plate motion is driven by heat-driven convection in the mantle, not rotation — rotation only affects things like the Coriolis effect.

items.correct: Large rocks rolling on the bed
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Large rocks rolling on the bed. “Tiny grains in the water” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Loss of river energy. “Strong rainfall” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many transport methods do rivers use?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
